mnemonics.title: Lean Seven Principles Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: E.L.E.P.T.D. = Eliminate waste, Learn amplify, Decide late, Expedite fast, Empower team, See whole, Deliver integrity. Each letter represents a key principle: Eliminate, Learn, Expedite, Empower, Decide, Deliver, See.

mnemonics.title: DSDM Eight Principles Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: A.I.D.E. T.N.D.C. = Active user involvement, Iterative development, Deliver on time, Emphasize business need, Never compromise quality, Iterate, Communicate visually, Demonstrate control. The acrostic organizes the eight principles into memorable groupings.

mnemonics.title: Crystal Family Scaling Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: C.Y.O.R. = Clear (1-8), Yellow (8-20), Orange (20-50), Red (50-300). Each progressive letter represents increasing team size and criticality. Remember: Crystal gets 'redder' (more intense) as project size grows.

mnemonics.title: FDD Five Activities Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: M.P.P.D.B. = Model develop, Plan feature, Design feature, Build feature (plus the initial feature list). The sequence flows from domain understanding through planning, design, and implementation.

acronyms.usage: Maximum number of items allowed in any workflow stage at once;核心机制 that enables pull-based flow in Kanban. Setting WIP limits requires balancing throughput against flow stability.
Agile methodology emphasizing domain modeling and feature-centric delivery through 5 activities. Chief programmer role provides technical leadership for feature teams.
Rigorous agile framework with eight principles, emphasizing timebox delivery and MoSCoW prioritization. Addresses projects requiring guaranteed delivery within fixed timeframes.

acronyms.expansion: Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have
acronyms.usage: DSDM prioritization technique ensuring focus on critical requirements. Rule of thumb: 60% maximum for Must Haves to enable realistic timebox management.
Fundamental flow equation derived from queuing theory. Essential for Kanban WIP limit calculations and predicting delivery times. For example, WIP=10, Throughput=2/day → Lead Time=5 days.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a subway map with stations (columns) and trains (work items). WIP limits are platform capacity—too many trains causes delays. Bottlenecks appear as stations with permanent backups. Flow improves when each station operates at optimal capacity.

Think of a spectrum from light (Clear) to dark (Red). As project criticality increases and team size grows, the methodology becomes more rigorous—more frequent check-ins, more documentation, stricter processes. It's like adjusting camera focus: small projects need loose focus, large critical projects need precise focus.

DSDM timeboxing is like a train schedule: the departure time (deadline) is fixed, and passengers (features) may be left behind if the train is full. Scrum sprints are like buses: you wait for the next one, but can extend the route if needed. DSDM never compromises on time; Scrum can adjust scope within fixed time.

Picture a factory assembly line with seven waste bins: 1) Partially done work (unfinished products), 2) Extra features (gold-plating), 3) Relearning (rework from unclear requirements), 4) Waiting (idle time), 5) Handoffs (excessive sign-offs), 6) Defects (bugs requiring rework), 7) Management overhead (unnecessary processes). Lean systematically eliminates these.

analogies.analogy: WIP limits are like a restaurant's table turnover. If a restaurant keeps adding customers to waiting tables (no WIP limit), service degrades for everyone. By limiting occupied tables (WIP), each gets proper attention, leading to faster overall service and higher customer satisfaction.

DSDM's time-first approach is like a news broadcast. The deadline is fixed (6 PM); if breaking news arrives late, less critical stories are dropped or shortened—not the broadcast time. Software delivery works identically: scope adjusts, not the deadline.

Deciding as late as possible is like buying a smartphone. Waiting until closer to purchase reveals better models and lower prices. Early decisions lock in assumptions that may become obsolete; late decisions incorporate more market information, reducing risk of choosing suboptimal solutions.

Crystal's methodology family is like clothing layers. A t-shirt (Crystal Clear) suffices for casual, small gatherings. A suit (Crystal Orange) is necessary for formal, larger events. Wearing a suit to a beach barbecue (over-apply rigor) or t-shirt to a board meeting (under-apply rigor) creates problems—matching methodology to context is essential.
